CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (neuralizing the entity grid, Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN, and incorporation of entity-specific features) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented consistently (convolutional architecture, pairwise ranking loss, training details and hyperparameters reported), and writing quality is good. Figures/tables and examples appear to support the empirical claims, though the summary suggests a few presentation gaps—e.g., limited discussion of which Extended Grid features were omitted and the exclusion of the Distributed Sentence model from results could be explained more explicitly for readers focused on interpretability and comparative baselines. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation to model to experiments and conclusion, with main contributions stated early (neuralized entity grid, CNN architecture, entity-specific features). Notation and methodological details (pairwise ranking loss, wide convolutions, optimization choices) are presented consistently and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, and the writing quality is generally good, though a couple of presentation gaps remain (e.g., exclusion of the distributed sentence model results and limited reporting on code availability), and the discussion of insertion-task limitations could be expanded for clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (neuralizing the entity grid, Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN, and incorporation of entity-specific features) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented consistently (convolutional architecture, pairwise ranking loss, training details and hyperparameters reported), and writing quality is good. Figures/tables and examples appear to support the empirical claims, though the summary suggests a few presentation gaps—e.g., limited discussion of which Extended Grid features were omitted and the exclusion of the Distributed Sentence model from results could be explained more explicitly for readers focused on interpretability and comparative baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation to model to experiments and conclusion, with main contributions stated early (neuralized entity grid, CNN architecture, entity-specific features). Notation and methodological details (pairwise ranking loss, wide convolutions, optimization choices) are presented consistently and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, and the writing quality is generally good, though a couple of presentation gaps remain (e.g., exclusion of the distributed sentence model results and limited reporting on code availability), and the discussion of insertion-task limitations could be expanded for clarity.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to several strong, relevant baselines (Elsner & Charniak extended/grid variants, Guinaudeau & Strube graph model) on the standard WSJ and DUC'03 evaluation metrics, which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, it omits reporting results for at least one competing neural baseline (Li & Hovy’s distributed sentence model was experimented with but excluded) and only implements a subset of Extended Grid features, limiting the directness of some comparisons; from a theory-first perspective I’d also expect explicit significance testing and more complete feature parity with symbolic baselines. | B(4): The paper compares to the core and recent coherence baselines (Elsner & Charniak grid variants, Guinaudeau & Strube graph model) on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03) using established metrics (discrimination, insertion, ranking). Comparisons appear fair and consistent, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods or tasks (insertion) remain challenging and note when they used only a subset of Extended Grid features. A minor omission is that the distributed sentence model (Li & Hovy, 2014) was experimented with but excluded from reported results, and code/reproducibility is not mentioned, so I withhold a perfect score despite overall strong and relevant empirical comparisons.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to several strong, relevant baselines (Elsner & Charniak extended/grid variants, Guinaudeau & Strube graph model) on the standard WSJ and DUC'03 evaluation metrics, which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, it omits reporting results for at least one competing neural baseline (Li & Hovy’s distributed sentence model was experimented with but excluded) and only implements a subset of Extended Grid features, limiting the directness of some comparisons; from a theory-first perspective I’d also expect explicit significance testing and more complete feature parity with symbolic baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to the core and recent coherence baselines (Elsner & Charniak grid variants, Guinaudeau & Strube graph model) on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03) using established metrics (discrimination, insertion, ranking). Comparisons appear fair and consistent, and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods or tasks (insertion) remain challenging and note when they used only a subset of Extended Grid features. A minor omission is that the distributed sentence model (Li & Hovy, 2014) was experimented with but excluded from reported results, and code/reproducibility is not mentioned, so I withhold a perfect score despite overall strong and relevant empirical comparisons.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper meaningfully advances prior entity-grid approaches by neuralizing the representation with a convolutional architecture and entity-specific embeddings, producing empirical gains. While the contribution is more an effective engineering and modeling extension than a radical theoretical shift, it is non-trivial and offers useful new modeling perspective and results, so a score slightly above mid-range is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear innovation in neuralizing the classic entity-grid via a convolutional architecture and incorporating entity-specific feature encodings, which is a notable engineering advance over prior symbolic/grid models. However, from a theory-first perspective this is an incremental methodological extension rather than a fundamentally new formulation: it applies standard CNN/ranking machinery to an existing representation without offering a formal generative or explanatory model of coherence or interpretability analyses of learned representations. The authors do differentiate their work from prior grid and graph models and show empirical gains, but the techniques are largely a non-trivial yet predictable extension of existing neuralization trends rather than a surprising conceptual leap.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clear and notable novelty by neuralizing the classic entity-grid through a convolutional architecture and training it end-to-end with a pairwise ranking loss, which meaningfully departs from prior hand-crafted transition models. Integrating entity-specific feature embeddings (named entity type, salience, proper mention) into the learned representation is a useful and non-trivial extension that an expert would find informative. While the overall problem (local coherence) is well-studied, the method offers a distinct modeling perspective and empirical gains on standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), so the contribution is more than incremental but not a radical paradigm shift.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is solid on standard coherence benchmarks and comparisons to key baselines, though the scope is somewhat limited and ablation/probing of learned representations is lacking. Given the clear empirical gains, reasonable reporting of hyperparameters, and candid discussion of limitations (e.g., subset of features used, smaller insertion gains), a score of 4 reflects strong contribution with modest gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to show empirical gains on standard coherence tasks (WSJ discrimination, DUC'03 rating) and compare against key symbolic baselines (graph-based, entity-grid variants), but the scope is limited — datasets are few and some prior methods (e.g., distributed sentence model) were excluded from final comparison. There is some sensitivity analysis (variants like Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN) but no detailed ablation or probing of learned representations to explain what linguistic patterns the CNN captures, and the model lacks a formal theoretical account of the inductive bias beyond architectural intuition. Reproduction details (hyperparameters reported) are helpful, but missing code and limited discussion of why insertion gains lag and which Extended Grid features were omitted reduce thoroughness.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: evaluations on standard WSJ discrimination and DUC’03 summary rating, plus comparisons to relevant baselines (Elsner & Charniak variants, Guinaudeau & Strube), support the main claims about Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN improvements. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited — although training details and hyperparameters are reported, there are no systematic ablations isolating architectural choices (e.g., convolution width, feature embeddings) beyond the note that only a subset of Extended Grid features were used. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the paper candidly discusses limitations (smaller gains on insertion, subset of features used), but code availability is not mentioned which reduces reproducibility despite reasonable implementation details. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong with minor gaps in ablations and full reproducibility.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a good fit for an NLP/ML venue, addressing local discourse coherence with appropriate methods, datasets, baselines, and evaluation. I rate it a 4 because while the empirical setup and positioning are solid, the work lacks deeper theoretical justification, broader empirical coverage, and some comparative choices that limit its scope — making it strong but not outstanding for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s topic—neuralizing the entity grid for local discourse coherence using CNNs and pairwise ranking—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and addresses the right community (discourse/coherence modeling). Methods and evaluation (WSJ/DUC datasets, discrimination/insertion/rating metrics, standard training details) meet venue expectations, and baselines include established grid and graph approaches. However, from a theory-first perspective the work lacks an explicit formal model or theoretical justification for its inductive biases, evaluation is limited to a small set of datasets (WSJ and DUC’03) and one variant of extended features, and some comparative choices (omitting the distributed sentence results) reduce its empirical breadth — concerns that make it a good but not perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP problem (local discourse coherence) and contributes a clear end-to-end neuralization of the widely-used entity grid using CNNs and jointly learned entity feature encodings. The experimental setup uses standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03), appropriate baselines (Elsner & Charniak variants, graph-based model), and standard evaluation metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) with reported hyperparameters and sensible optimization choices, matching community expectations. The work is aimed squarely at the NLP/ML audience interested in neural discourse models and is positioned well with thorough related work coverage and clear limitations noted.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and delivers a practically useful, empirically validated neuralization of the entity grid with consistent gains on standard benchmarks. Concerns about limited theoretical analysis, narrower experimental scope, and incomplete feature/code release temper claims of broad impact, but the clear empirical improvements and likely usability make this work more than merely incremental and likely to influence follow-on work, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important, well-motivated problem—neuralizing the entity grid for local coherence and demonstrates practical gains with Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN on standard tasks (WSJ discrimination, DUC’03 summary rating, insertion). However, from a theory-first perspective the contribution lacks a formal model or inductive-bias justification for the convolutional architecture and provides little analysis or probing of what the learned representations capture. The experimental scope is also limited (primarily WSJ and DUC’03) and some Extended Grid features were omitted, constraining claims about generality; thus it is a useful, incrementally valuable contribution but not field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, well-studied problem (local coherence) and provides a practically appealing end-to-end neuralization of the entity grid that embeds entity-specific features—an approach I reward—showing consistent empirical gains (Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN) on standard benchmarks (WSJ, DUC'03) and multiple metrics. The method’s integration of entity features into a learned convolutional architecture, clear training details and ablation-style choices make it likely to influence subsequent work and be built upon, though the lack of reported code and only partial feature coverage for the extended grid temper its broader transformative potential.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are supported by end-to-end experiments with sensible baselines and reported hyperparameters, and that the mathematical setup is adequate. Remaining concerns (limited datasets, partial implementation of Extended Grid features, lack of theoretical analysis and significance testing) are important but do not fundamentally undermine the empirical results, so a score slightly above average is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims (Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN outperform existing baselines on discrimination and summary rating) are supported by experiments on WSJ and DUC’03 using standard metrics, and hyperparameters and training details are reported. However, there is no formal theoretical model explaining the inductive bias of the convolutional architecture or analyses/probing of learned representations to justify interpretability claims; Extended Grid features were only partially reproduced, and the experimental scope is limited to two primary datasets with no mention of statistical significance testing. These gaps create moderate concerns about the generality and explanatory validity of the claims, though they do not invalidate the reported empirical improvements.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with end-to-end experiments showing Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN outperform prior grid-based baselines on discrimination and summary coherence ranking, and reports hyperparameters and training details. Mathematical content is lightweight and correct for the CNN/ranking setup; there are no formal proofs needed. Experimental methodology is largely sound—relevant baselines are included and sensible controls/optimization choices are reported—but some concerns remain: insertion gains are smaller and the model was not trained specifically for that task, the Extended Grid-CNN only implements a subset of prior handcrafted features, and the summary omits mention of code release and statistical-significance testing. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with only minor issues that do not undermine the main contributions.

main_claims: Proposes a neural local coherence model that neuralizes the entity grid using a convolutional architecture to model long-range entity transitions.
The model can incorporate entity-specific features (e.g., named entity type, salience, proper mention) within the neural framework.

Trained with a pairwise ranking objective, the proposed Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN outperform existing baselines on coherence assessment tasks (discrimination, insertion, and summary coherence rating).
_summary.methodology: A convolutional neural network is applied over entity-grid representations (with optional entity-specific feature encodings) and trained end-to-end using a pairwise ranking loss to score document coherence; the model uses wide convolutions, dropout, early stopping, and standard weight initialization/optimization schemes.
datasets: Wall Street Journal (WSJ) portion of the Penn Treebank
AIRPLANES (mentioned as used by prior work)
EARTHQUAKES (mentioned as used by prior work)
baselines: Distributed sentence model (Li & Hovy, 2014) (experimented with but excluded from results)
limitations: Gains on the insertion task are smaller than on discrimination; authors note insertion is harder and their model was not trained specifically for insertion.
Extended Grid-CNN experiments included only a subset of Extended Grid features (three easy-to-compute features) rather than all features used by Elsner & Charniak.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - local discourse coherence modeling / entity-grid neuralization

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Linguistic ML
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a focus on formal models of discourse and linguistic theory integration into ML. Has authored work on formal coherence metrics and theoretical analyses of discourse models.
traits: penalizes papers that present empirical gains without a formal model or theoretical justification for their inductive biases (deduct 1–2 points on novelty/impact when missing)
requires explicit interpretability or analysis of learned representations (deduct points if no probing/visualization of what the model captures)
penalizes evaluations limited to ≤2 datasets or lacking clear statistical significance testing (deduct when experimental scope is narrow)
demands comparison to strong symbolic/linguistic baselines (deduct if only neural baselines are used)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes theoretical clarity, interpretability, and rigorous comparative evaluation; skeptical of purely empirical incremental work without explanatory insight.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Systems Optimist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in NLP with extensive experience building and evaluating end-to-end neural models for document-level tasks; focuses on practical performance, model engineering, and robust empirical evaluation.
traits: rewards clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (add 1–2 points when model improves prior baselines in multiple metrics/datasets)
favors end-to-end neural architectures that integrate traditionally hand-crafted features into learned representations (bonus when entity features are embedded and trained jointly)
rewards thorough ablation studies and sensible optimization choices (adds confidence/points if ablations and hyperparameter tuning are reported)
prefers reproducible setups: values when code, training details, and datasets are clearly described (adds points when reproducibility is addressed)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Values strong empirical evidence, practical model contributions, and reproducibility; inclined to accept well-executed neural approaches that show consistent gains.
